name: WPW — Wolff-Parkinson-White
identifyBy: Short PR interval <120ms (pre-excitation — impulse bypasses AV node)
Delta wave: slurred upstroke at onset of QRS (initial slow conduction via accessory pathway)
Pseudo ST/T changes secondary to abnormal depolarisation
Secondary ST-T changes: ST depression and T inversion in leads with dominant delta waves
management: Asymptomatic WPW: cardiology referral for risk stratification
Symptomatic WPW (SVT, AF, syncope): urgent cardiology referral for EP study
AVOID: Digoxin, Verapamil, Adenosine (in AF) — may preferentially conduct AF via pathway
If presenting with WPW + AF: treat as pre-excited AF (see separate entry)
pearls: Delta wave polarity localises the accessory pathway: positive V1 = left-sided, negative V1 = right-sided
Up to 25% of WPW is asymptomatic — found incidentally
Risk of sudden death: ~1 in 1000 patient-years — due to rapid AF conducting via accessory pathway to ventricles
Lown-Ganong-Levine (LGL) syndrome: short PR, narrow QRS, no delta wave — different pathway location
WPW on ECG mimics MI: negative delta waves in inferior/anterior leads simulate pathological Q waves
differentials: LBBB/RBBB (wide QRS but no delta wave, no short PR)
Inferior MI with Q waves (delta wave mimics Q)
